evidence.page_title: Why is the Golden Gate Bridge so famous? What people usually miss
evidence.title: Why is the Golden Gate Bridge so famous? What people usually miss
evidence.description: It stands there in the fog. Huge. International Orange. Most people think they know the answer to **why is the Golden Gate Bridge so famous**, but honestly,...
evidence.text:
It stands there in the fog. Huge. International Orange. Most people think they know the answer to why is the Golden Gate Bridge so famous, but honestly, the postcard version barely scratches the surface. It’s not just a big orange bridge in California. If that were true, people wouldn’t fly ten thousand miles just to see a hunk of steel.
It’s about the fact that it was never supposed to be built.
Back in the 1920s, experts said it was impossible. The currents were too strong. The water was too deep. The wind would tear anything apart. They called the entrance to the San Francisco Bay "the graveyard of ships." And yet, here we are. It’s a miracle of grit that shouldn't exist, wrapped in a color that was actually just supposed to be a primer.
The engineering "suicide mission" that actually worked
To understand the fame, you have to look at the sheer audacity of the construction. Joseph Strauss, the chief engineer, wasn't even a suspension bridge expert at first. He was a guy who built drawbridges. But he was a visionary. Or maybe just stubborn.
In 1933, the Great Depression was strangling the country. Most projects were dying. But San Francisco went ahead anyway. They didn't even use federal money. They put up their own homes and businesses as collateral for the bonds. That’s how much they wanted this thing. Imagine that today—regular people risking their houses to build a bridge. It’s wild.
The conditions were brutal. Divers had to go down into the freezing, churning waters of the Golden Gate Strait with no lights and heavy brass helmets to blast away rock. They could only work when the tide turned, which gave them about twenty minutes a day. If they stayed down too long, the current would literally rip their air hoses off.
Safety was the real game changer
One reason this bridge is a legend in the engineering world is because of a giant net. Strauss was a stickler for safety. He spent $130,000 on a massive safety net hung under the floor of the bridge. It was a revolutionary move.
- It saved 19 men.
- These guys called themselves the "Halfway to Hell Club."
- Until near the end, they had an almost perfect safety record.
Sadly, 11 men still died, ten of them in a single horrific accident when a scaffold fell through the net. But compared to other projects of the era, where one death per million dollars spent was the "norm," the Golden Gate was a pioneer in keeping workers alive.
That specific shade of "International Orange"
Let’s talk about the color. Why is it that specific, weirdly beautiful orange?
Most people assume it was a genius design choice from day one. It wasn't. The U.S. Navy actually wanted it painted in black and yellow stripes. They wanted it to look like a giant bumblebee so ships could see it in the fog. Can you imagine how ugly that would have been?
The "International Orange" we see today was actually the color of the protective sealant the steel arrived in. Irving Morrow, the consulting architect, saw it and realized it looked incredible against the blue of the water and the green of the Marin headlands. He had to fight for it. He wrote a 25-page report just to convince the board that "boring gray" or "bumblebee stripes" would ruin the vibe. He won.
It’s famous because it glows. When the San Francisco fog rolls in—which it does, constantly—the bridge doesn't disappear. It peeks through. That contrast between the organic, shifting mist and the rigid, vibrant orange steel is a photographer’s dream. It’s basically the original "Instagrammable" location before the internet existed.
It was the biggest... for a while
Size matters. For a long time, the Golden Gate Bridge held the title of the longest suspension bridge span in the world. From 1937 until 1964, its 4,200-foot main span was the undisputed king. It was eventually beaten by the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge in New York, and later by monsters in Japan and Turkey like the 1915 Çanakkale Bridge.
But records are funny. Once you're the "first" truly iconic giant, people don't care if a bigger one comes along. It’s like the Empire State Building. It’s not the tallest anymore, but it’s the skyscraper. The Golden Gate is the bridge.
The Art Deco flair
Look closely at the towers. They aren't just flat steel pillars. They have these stepped-back silhouettes. That’s Art Deco influence. Most bridges are purely functional. They look like machines. The Golden Gate looks like a piece of jewelry.
Morrow added vertical ribbing on the towers so that when the sun hits them, they create shadows that emphasize the height. It’s a masterpiece of "form meeting function." It doesn't just hold up cars; it looks like it’s reaching for the sky.
Why it still captures the world's imagination
Pop culture did a lot of the heavy lifting. If you want to destroy a landmark in a movie, you pick the Golden Gate. Magneto moved it in X-Men. Godzilla walked through it. It’s been destroyed by aliens, earthquakes, and giant sharks.
Why? Because it represents the edge of the world.
For decades, San Francisco was the "Gateway to the Pacific." If you were leaving the United States for the West, this was the last thing you saw. If you were coming home from a war in the Pacific, this was the first sign of soil. It’s a psychological boundary.
The darker side of the fame
We can't talk about why is the Golden Gate Bridge so famous without mentioning the grim reality of its reputation. It is one of the most used suicide sites in the world. It’s a heavy, sad part of its history that the city has struggled with for decades.
After years of debate about aesthetics versus safety, they finally installed a suicide deterrent net. It’s nearly invisible from a distance, but it’s there now. It's a reminder that landmarks aren't just pretty shapes; they are places where real, often messy, human life happens.
Surprising facts you can use to impress people
- The cables are terrifyingly long. If you took all the steel wires in the two main cables and laid them end-to-end, they would circle the Earth three times.
- It’s constantly being painted. People think they paint it start-to-finish then start over. That's a myth. It’s actually a localized process based on which parts are corroding the fastest from the salt air.
- The 50th Anniversary was a disaster. In 1987, they opened the bridge to pedestrians. So many people showed up (about 300,000) that the weight actually flattened the arch of the bridge. It groaned. People panicked. Engineers later said the bridge was fine, but it was the most stress the structure had ever seen.
- The fog has a name. Locals call it Karl. Karl the Fog often completely hides the bridge, which only adds to the mystery.
How to actually experience it (The right way)
If you're going there, don't just drive across it. You'll miss everything.
- Walk the East Sidewalk. It’s loud because of the traffic, but the view of the city skyline is unbeatable. Just hold onto your hat; the wind is no joke.
- Go to Battery Spencer. This is the spot on the Marin side (north) where everyone takes those "perfect" photos. It’s an old military fort. It’s high up. It’s windy. It’s perfect.
- Check the fog tracker. Seriously, look at the weather. If it’s "socked in," you won't see a thing. Sometimes that’s cool, but usually, you want at least a glimpse of the towers.
- Rent a bike in Fisherman's Wharf. Ride across the bridge and take the ferry back from Sausalito. It’s the classic tourist move for a reason—it’s actually fun.
The Actionable Insight
If you're studying the bridge for school, planning a trip, or just curious, remember that its fame comes from a "perfect storm" of three things: impossibility, color, and location.
It shouldn't have been built, it shouldn't have been orange, and it shouldn't be as beautiful as it is given how much salt and wind hit it every day.
For your next step, look up the "Halfway to Hell Club" or check out the live bridge cams. Seeing the ships pass under that 220-foot clearance in real-time gives you a perspective on the scale that no photo can. If you're visiting, book a ferry tour that goes under the bridge. Seeing the underside of the suspension deck is the only way to truly feel how small we are compared to the "impossible" things humans can build.

evidence.page_title: People Who Hate The Golden Gate Bridge
evidence.title: People Who Hate The Golden Gate Bridge
evidence.description:
The Golden Gate Bridge is iconic. It’s a legend, a thing of beauty, the star of the show, a marvel, and everything in between. When I lived in Boston for a short period of time for college, I would come home from college and swoon every time I’d see that

The post People Who Hate The Golden Gate Bridge appeared first on Broke-Ass Stuart's Website.
evidence.text:
The Golden Gate Bridge is iconic. It’s a legend, a thing of beauty, the star of the show, a marvel, and everything in between. When I lived in Boston for a short period of time for college, I would come home from college and swoon every time I’d see that beautiful bridge from the car window. It symbolizes home for me. It’s hard to explain. Each of us locals probably feel similarly about some piece of the landscape here in The Bay.
I wondered… “Does anyone hate the Golden Gate Bridge?”
The Golden Gate Bridge 50th Anniversary Bridge Walk in May 1987 – photo from SFGate by Ed Perlstein/BayArea80s
Do you remember during the 50th birthday of the bridge when they shut it all down and the sheer number of people on top of the bridge flattened the thing? Or when those teens climbed to the top just before the GGB’s 80th birthday and were playing on the cables like it was no big deal? We love our bridge for sure.
Turns out. Many people find it to be a tourist attraction worth skipping when visiting and have written some hilariously mean reviews. I find their reasoning to be pretty hilarious and worth sharing.
Reviews complaining about The Golden Gate Bride’s classic color:
I’d say a bulk of the complaints are on the color of the bridge. Just for those who aren’t sure why it’s called the Golden Gate Bridge. Well, the name is actually not from the color. Rather, the name comes from the Golden Gate Strait which is the stretch of water below the bridge that links the Pacific Ocean to the inner portion of the bay.
“I’m not a fan of the color red. Also, the thing is just freakin’ huge, ya know. Just take the long way around it,” – Michael R.
“This “bridge” was neither golden nor a gate. I can understand why some people might find it useful for commuting, however it is just not for me. In the future I will choose to swim across the bay.”- Colby W.
Derek D, says, “Hated it, I mean really it’s red not golden, a total disappointment. Thinking I’m going to ask my travel agent for a partial refund. It does though, make a pretty good bridge.”
“The Meh Bridge. Not golden, no gate, short, not nearly as big as pictures make it look,” boasts the Google user Lisa Lindemann.
Wonder if Lisa actually walked across, too? Personally, I get across half of it and I’m exhausted. I suppose 1.7 miles isn’t that long but The Brooklyn Bridge is only 1.1 miles long. When built in the 30s, it was the longest suspension bridge ever built.
Construction of the Golden Gate Bridge in October 1935 – photo from Getty Images
The Golden Gate Bridge isn’t special enough for some:
Jared H. from Hawaii may not have ever seen a bridge before or more likely he was trying to be funny when he said on Yelp,
“The bridge just….. sat there. It didn’t do anything. I thought it would be more fun. They put nets on the sides to prevent people jumping off to go for a nice cool swim.”
We all know living in The Bay Area costs a ton. We can also all agree that toll here is definitely pretty damn expensive. That being said, this review by Anoose Killmore gave me a bit of a chuckle,
“Literally just a bridge that’s expensive to cross. Nothing pretty about a dirty tourist trap.”
If anything, these reviews thought me that beauty might be in the eye of the beholder… naw, they just taught me that those people are ridiculous. Most reviews are overwhelmingly positive, obviously. Our GGB’s beauty is everlasting and we will all always feel more at home when we gaze upon those golden red pillars.
Like this post? Then you’ll LOVE Stuart’s new zine!
Get this limited edition, hand signed zine covering 20 years of Stuart’s writings on San Francisco! Learn more right here.
The post People Who Hate The Golden Gate Bridge appeared first on Broke-Ass Stuart's Website.

evidence.page_title: 32 years ago, 300,000 people flattened the Golden Gate Bridge
evidence.title: 32 years ago, 300,000 people flattened the Golden Gate Bridge
evidence.description: Friday marked the 32nd anniversary of the walk across the Golden Gate Bridge, an event The Chronicle's Peter Hartlaub once referred to as "the largest cluster— in Bay Area history where no one...
evidence.text:
Friday marked the 32nd anniversary of the walk across the Golden Gate Bridge, an event The Chronicle's Peter Hartlaub once referred to as "the largest clusterf— in Bay Area history where no one actually died."
The May 24, 1987 event celebrating the bridge's 50th anniversary was organized by the "Friends of the Golden Gate Bridge," a group made up of five members of the bridge district board of directors. The group expected a crowd of 80,000 people, but instead received an estimated 800,000 people at the event.
Advertisement
Article continues below this ad
Approximately 300,000 people actually engaged in the walk across the bridge, an experience that — in the most generous terms — could be described as "extremely unpleasant."
"While trapped shoulder to shoulder in the mob, unable to move for more than two hours, I remember discussing with my wife the real possibility that we were about to participate in one of the 20th century's landmark disasters," San Francisco painting and plastering contractor Winston Montgomery recounted 10 years after the event. "A bridge collapse would have put to shame all those petty Third World bus and ferry tragedies you read about in the newspaper."
See more S.F. Chronicle on Google
The weight of the 300,000 people flattened the normally arched roadbed, although bridge engineers later said there was never any real danger.
Advertisement
Article continues below this ad
"The Golden Gate Bridge, all 419,000 tons of it, groaned and swayed like an old wooden plank thrown across a ditch," Montgomery wrote. "Frightened and seasick people vomited on their shoes."
According to Montgomery, people began throwing bicycles and strollers off the bridge in order to lighten the load on the bridge.
"There were cheers as some people started to hurl bicycles over the railing," he wrote. "A stroller tumbled down and sank beneath the waves 220 feet below. 'Throw the baby, too,' people yelled, laughing. These were probably the bridge walkers trapped on the sidewalk of a roadbed 95 feet wide, with only a 4-foot railing between them and eternity."
While there were some injuries, there were no fatalities and the bridge was not damaged.
Advertisement
Article continues below this ad
Click through the slideshow above to see photos of the 1987 walk across the Golden Gate Bridge.
Eric Ting is an SFGATE staff writer. Email: eric.ting@sfgate.com | Twitter:@_ericting

image_captions: The Golden Gate Bridge 50th Anniversary Bridge Walk in May 1987.Ed Perlstein/BayArea80s
Hundreds of thousands of people crowded onto the Golden Gate Bridge to celebrate its 50th anniversary. San Francisco, California, May 24, 1987.Visions of America LLC/Corbis via Getty Images

May 24, 1987 - Shot from the South Tower facing south down onto the roadway overlooking a mass of people at the 50th anniversary celebration for the Golden Gate Bridge.Deanne Fitzmaurice/The Chronicle
Mass of people gather at the south end of the Golden Gate Bridge for the 50th anniversary celebration on May 24, 1987.Frederic Larson/The Chronicle
May 24, 1987: Billie Mitchell of Kelsey, Calif., who was 16 when the Golden Gate Bridge opened, returns on the 50th anniversary with family in tow. (Tom Levy / The Chronicle)
